champion.narrative: No champion has been identified. Amy Shead (Senior Category Manager, Procurement Center of Excellence) is the sole contact role, tagged Decision Maker, and is the only named buyer stakeholder across Salesforce and Avoma (no calls discovered). Her last contact date is 04 Jun 2026 (31 days ago, per LastActivityDate), and there is no evidence of recent engagement, advocacy, or access to the Economic Buyer or procurement leadership. A champion must exhibit: (1) access to the EB/DM and willingness to introduce Zycus, (2) active advocacy (pushing Zycus internally, sharing competitive intel, driving the timeline), (3) aligned interest (their success metrics improve if Zycus wins). Amy's role as category manager suggests she is an end user or process owner, not a budget authority or executive sponsor. No evidence exists that she has introduced Zycus to finance, IT, or procurement leadership (the Next Step agenda references aligning these groups, but no meetings or introductions have occurred). No Salesforce field indicates champion development: X10b_Champion_Business_Buyer__c is null or not in org, and no Champion contact role exists beyond the Decision Maker tag on Amy. The 31-day silence since 04 Jun, combined with zero multi-threading and zero buyer calls, suggests Amy is either (1) not a champion (passive, awaiting vendor follow-up, no internal advocacy), (2) a developing champion who has stalled (interested but lacking urgency or authority to move the deal), or (3) disengaged (ghosting, deal deprioritized). The immediate move is to re-engage Amy within 7 days, assess her willingness to advocate and introduce Zycus to the buying committee, and if she is passive or lacks authority, identify and cultivate a true champion (a procurement leader, category director, or CFO-office sponsor with EB access and urgency).

competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is named as the competitor (SF Competitors__c), but no competitive intelligence exists on Ariba's role, threat level, or Zycus's wedge strategy. Ariba is the dominant enterprise P2P/sourcing platform and is widely deployed across Southern Company (Southern Nuclear's parent) for indirect procurement, so Ariba may be: (1) the incumbent (Southern Nuclear already uses Ariba, and this is a displacement or module-replacement opportunity), (2) a bake-off competitor (Southern is evaluating Ariba vs Zycus for a new deployment or refresh), or (3) ruled out (Ariba was considered but eliminated, and Zycus is now sole-source). The Products field lists 'ML iAnalyze;iSupplier;eProcurement;Merlin,' suggesting a full-suite P2P + analytics + AI play, which overlaps heavily with the relevant stakeholder, Contract Management, and Spend Visibility modules. If Ariba is the incumbent, displacing it requires a wedge: (1) Merlin AI for negotiation automation and sourcing intelligence (Ariba lacks agentic AI for supplier negotiation), (2) tail-spend and category-management depth (iAnalyze for non-PO spend visibility, a common Ariba weak spot), (3) usability and adoption (Zycus eProcurement is often positioned as simpler/faster than Ariba's UI), (4) cost (Zycus may be 30-40% lower TCO than Ariba for equivalent modules). However, none of this is buyer-confirmed. No Avoma calls exist to reveal Ariba's positioning, buyer sentiment toward Ariba, or Ariba's response to the relevant stakeholder fields contain competitive detail: How_are_you_addressing_your_problem_toda__c (current stack), Existing_vendor__c, Replacing_What__c, Why_not_Zycus__c, Zycus_Differentiation_Why_Zycus__c are null or not in org. The Next Step agenda references 'Confirm current tools for spend reporting, sourcing, and supplier management,' signaling the rep will map the incumbent stack during discovery, but that has not occurred. The risk: if Ariba is the entrenched incumbent with a multi-year enterprise agreement, deep integrations to the relevant stakeholder, and executive sponsorship, displacing it is a 12-18 month political and technical battle, and the 01 Oct close date is fantasy. Discovery must map Ariba's footprint (modules in use, user satisfaction, contract expiration, pain points), confirm whether this is a displacement or net-new opportunity, and position the Merlin + iAnalyze wedge as the reason to switch or augment.

identify_pain.narrative: No specific operational or financial pain has been articulated by the buyer. The Next Step agenda (18 Jun) lists discovery objectives that imply the rep's hypothesis of pain areas: 'current gaps' in category management, 'supplier concentration' risk, 'current tools for spend reporting, sourcing, and supplier management' (suggesting dissatisfaction or limitation with existing systems). However, these are the rep's discovery questions, not confirmed buyer pain. No Salesforce fields contain pain statements: Customer_Business_Problem__c, Pain_points_in_the_current_solution__c, What_if_this_is_not_done__c, Compelling_Event__c are null or not in org. No Avoma calls exist to quote buyer pain. For a nuclear utility P2P/SRM deal, common pain profiles include: (1) tail-spend visibility gap (non-PO spend, maverick buying outside Ariba or ERP, leading to cost leakage and compliance risk), (2) supplier concentration risk (over-reliance on single suppliers for critical MRO parts, a material risk for plant ops), (3) category-sourcing inefficiency (manual RFx processes, slow cycle time, weak analytics for cost-down), (4) Ariba complexity or cost (if Ariba is incumbent, users may find it heavy/slow, or Southern may seek to reduce Ariba module spend by replacing with Zycus), (5) lack of AI-driven sourcing or negotiation capability (Merlin opportunity). Without a named pain owner (a procurement leader, category manager, or CFO office stakeholder who owns the problem and its cost/impact), the deal lacks urgency and a champion catalyst. Discovery must elicit the pain in the buyer's words, quantify it (cost, time, risk exposure), and name the pain owner. For example: 'Our tail-spend compliance is 60%, we're leaking $2M/year to maverick POs, and our CPO has mandated 80% compliance by Q4' (pain owner: CPO, quantified impact: $2M, urgency: Q4 mandate). Until that level of specificity exists, pain is a gap.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics and paper process are completely unknown. No Salesforce fields reference legal review, MSA/SOW status, procurement channel, or InfoSec requirements. Current_Contract_Expiration__c is null (no expiration forcing function). No Avoma calls exist to discuss contracting. For a regulated nuclear utility (Southern Nuclear operates Vogtle, Hatch, Farley plants under NRC oversight), paper process is typically lengthy and prescriptive: (1) IT security review (cloud SaaS vendor assessment, SOC2/ISO certification, data residency, NRC cybersecurity posture if procurement-system data touches plant ops or vendor risk), (2) legal review (MSA redlines for liability, indemnity, data ownership, compliance with the relevant stakeholder corporate legal standards), (3) procurement policy compliance (competitive bidding if required, vendor registration, insurance certificates), (4) finance sign-off (budget code, multi-year amortization for $100k). If SAP Ariba is the incumbent, an MSA may already exist (Ariba is widely deployed at Southern Company for indirect procurement), and displacing Ariba means a NEW vendor MSA + SOW, adding 4-6 weeks to the cycle. If an SI or implementation partner is involved (Products field includes Merlin, which may require configuration/training services), SOW scoping for services is a separate gate. The 01 Oct close date allows ~4 weeks for contracting (01 Sep signature target) if negotiation completes by late Aug, but this is aggressive for a first-time vendor at a regulated Fortune 500 utility. Discovery and demo phases must surface the paper requirements early (Who owns vendor onboarding? What is the MSA template? Is InfoSec review triggered above $X spend or for all cloud SaaS? Is an SI required for implementation?) so the deal team can pre-position legal, security, and procurement collateral and avoid a late-cycle stall.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is unknown. Discovery must assess AI appetite (e.g. 'Are you evaluating AI for sourcing or negotiation automation?') and, if positive, make Merlin the centerpiece of the demo (agentic negotiation on supplier price history, AI-driven RFx, contract intelligence).
